basis.reasoning: The answer is synthesized from multiple technical documentation sources and community forums. The core claim that SonarQube blocks iframes via X-Frame-Options set to SAMEORIGIN is supported by Stack Overflow discussions and Tutorialpedia. The necessity of adjusting cookie attributes is based on MDN Web Docs for Third-party cookies, while the use of the Storage Access API for cross-site iframe cookies is supported by MDN's Storage Access API documentation. The transition to CSP frame-ancestors is backed by MDN Web Docs. Furthermore, SonarQube's JWT token mechanism for session persistence during startups is directly supported by Sonar Documentation. However, the report includes several significant architectural recommendations—such as implementing a PostMessage token bridge for CSRF, using specific sandbox attributes like allow-modals, and performance optimizations like lazy-loading and DNS-prefetching—that completely lack support in the provided citations. Because these major implementation details are unverified, confidence is medium.

excerpts: This differs from frame-src, which allows you to specify where iframes in a page may be loaded from. Note: The frame-ancestors directive checks each ancestor. If any ancestor doesn't match, the load is cancelled. Therefore all ancestors should be allowed by the frame-ancestors directive of leaf frames when using nested frames.
citations.title: http://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Headers/Content-Security-Policy/frame-ancestors

excerpts: CSP frame-ancestors can only restrict framing, so setting it won't make it easier to load. It is not clear on which of the pages you set the CSP. If A frames B then frame-ancestors on B will determine if A is allowed to frame the content, while frame-ancestors only on A will have no impact. You should make your question more clear. Finally it is 'self' with single quotes.
citations.title: iframe - content security policy frame-ancestors - Stack Overflow

excerpts: While critical for security, SAMEORIGIN can also block legitimate embedding, like displaying a subdomain’s content or a partner site’s page. Understanding X-Frame-Options What is X-Frame-Options? X-Frame-Options is an HTTP response header introduced to protect websites from clickjacking.

If you’ve ever tried to embed a webpage into another site using an `<iframe>`, you might have encountered a frustrating error: *“Refused to display ‘ [URL]’ in a frame because it set ‘X-Frame-Options’ to ‘SAMEORIGIN’.”*
citations.title: How to Fix 'Refused to Display in a Frame' Error: X-Frame ...

excerpts: Directives DENY The document cannot be loaded in any frame, regardless of origin (both same- and cross-origin embedding is blocked). SAMEORIGIN The document can only be embedded if all ancestor frames have the same origin as the page itself. ALLOW-FROM origin This is an obsolete directive. Modern browsers that encounter response headers with this directive will ignore the header completely
citations.title: X-Frame-Options header - HTTP | MDN - MDN Web Docs

citations.title: cross domain - Iframe content blocked - Stack Overflow

excerpts: The Storage Access API provides a way for cross-site content loaded in a third-party context (i.e., embedded in an `<iframe>` ) to gain access to third-party cookies and unpartitioned state that it would typically only have access to in a first-party context (i.e., when loaded directly in a browser tab).
The Storage Access API is only needed to provide access to _unpartitioned_ third-party cookies!

Although the API surface is the same, websites using the Storage Access API should expect differences in the level and extent of third-party cookie access they receive between different browsers, due to differences in their storage access policies.
citations.title: Storage Access API - MDN Web Docs

excerpts: Optional pre-installation step to keep user sessions alive during startup.

By default, users are logged out and sessions closed when server is restarted. If you prefer keeping user sessions open, a secret should be defined. Value is HS256 key encoded with base64. It must be unique for each installation of SonarQube Server.
citations.title: Defining a JWT token | SonarQube Server | Sonar Documentation
